retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Coronary Intervention -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous coronary intervention is performed during an angiogram in the angiography suite. Other than standard equipment, the following supplies may be used on a case-to-case basis. Introducer needle Sheath introducer Guide catheters Radioopaque dye (IV contrast) Guidewire Balloon catheter Stents Bare metal stents (BMS) Preferred if the patient is not able to take a longer duration of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT). A minimum of 1 month of DAPT is required. Drug-eluting stents (DES) Found to have reduced restenosis and revascularization rates compared with BMS. Bioresorbable vascular scaffold (BVS) Drug-eluting balloons (DEB) Thrombus aspiration: Not recommended routinely Guidelines Atherectomy devices Rotational Laser Cutting balloons Radiation beads Lithoplasty/lithotripsy systems

retrieved_docs.content: Make sure the double J and guidewire are both very moist if using hydrophilic materials; otherwise, it can be challenging to advance the stent over the guidewire. Most hydrophilic guidewires and stents dry out very quickly and then become sticky.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty and Balloon Catheters -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Femoral artery: After palpation of the common femoral artery, 2cm below the center of the inguinal ligament at the point of maximum palpable impulse, an arteriotomy incision is made. Fluoroscopy can be used for better localization of the femoral artery, seen in the middle of the femoral head. Subsequently, a micropuncture needle is used to enter the femoral artery, and a hydrophilic sheath is placed with a modified Selinger technique.

retrieved_docs.title: Transluminal Extraction Coronary Atherectomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Several trials have defined the optimal use of PTRA. The Study to Determine Rotablator and Transluminal Angioplasty Strategy (STRATAS) trial compared an aggressive debulking strategy (burr/artery ratio of 0.7 to 0.9 followed by balloon inflation of less than 1 atm or no inflation) with a moderate debulking strategy (burr/artery ratio of less than 0.7 followed by conventional balloon angioplasty). The clinical success was similar, but the aggressive strategy caused more myocardial infarctions (11% vs. 7%) and a higher rate of restenosis (58% vs. 52%). [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Restenosis of Stented Coronary Arteries -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cutting and scoring balloons: These balloons work by incising neointimal tissue, thereby allowing for better balloon anchoring during angioplasty. The cutting or scoring action also helps to prep the lesion before balloon angioplasty to allow for better tissue extrusion. However, the largest randomized trial to date to compare cutting balloon angioplasty to POBA in the treatment of ISR, Restenosis Cutting Balloon Evaluation (RESCUT) Trial, failed to show improvement in angiographic stenosis or the rate of clinical events during its seven-month follow-up. Cutting balloon angioplasty correlated with a need to use fewer balloons, less additional stenting, and a lower rate of balloon slippage. The ISAR-DESIRE IV trial (Intracoronary Stenting and Angiographic Results: Optimizing Treatment of Drug-Eluting Stent In-Stent Restenosis 4) subsequently evaluated the use of scoring balloons before paclitaxel-coated balloon angioplasty for treatment of DES-ISR. The trial showed superior angiographic outcomes at six months in the scoring balloon arm but was underpowered to show any significant difference in clinical outcomes. At this time, there are no large randomized trials to show improvement in clinical outcomes with the use of either cutting or scoring balloons in the treatment of ISR. Their use may be a consideration in those situations where balloon migration is of particular concern as well as with the presence of significant tissue. [8] [32] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty and Balloon Catheters -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Angioplasty procedures are a minimally invasive alternative to an open surgical approach for arterial stenosis. As a result, these procedures carry significantly lower morbidity and mortality with a shorter duration of hospital stay and recovery with an acceptable risk of restenosis. Percutaneous coronary angioplasty has revolutionized the treatment of MI and unstable angina and now is the standard of care. Stenting and angioplasty are an acceptable alternative for peripheral vascular disease for suitable indications and have better recovery rates at the cost of lower revascularization rates. Carotid angioplasty procedures, however, have more risk than benefit in comparison to carotid endarterectomy procedures and are advisable only in a select group of patients. The overall success of the interventions is also enhanced by patient compliance with diet, exercise, medications, smoking cessation, and medical optimization of comorbidities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 926
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass a range of catheter‑based interventions designed to treat coronary artery disease, most notably percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) and coronary thrombolysis. Both ptca and coronary thrombolysis are classified as forms of interventional cardiac catheterization, indicating that they are performed through vascular access to the coronary circulation. Ptca is a mechanical technique used to dilate narrowed coronary segments and is a specific subcategory within the broader Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures. Coronary thrombolysis, which employs pharmacologic agents to dissolve thrombotic occlusions, is also a component of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and serves the purpose of removal of coronary artery obstruction. A recognized clinical sequela of ptca is the occurrence of recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, underscoring the importance of post‑procedure monitoring and possible additional therapeutic measures. Together, these interventional cardiac catheterization procedures provide complementary strategies for restoring coronary patency and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 839
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass a range of interventional techniques used to treat coronary artery disease. Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) is classified as a specific type of both Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and interventional cardiac catheterization, reflecting its role as a catheter‑based, minimally invasive revascularization method. The Arabic description “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد” corresponds to ptca, indicating the same procedure in another language. Coronary thrombolysis, another modality within Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures, is also categorized under interventional cardiac catheterization, highlighting its use of catheter‑delivered pharmacologic agents to dissolve thrombus. While ptca effectively restores vessel patency, patients may experience recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, underscoring the need for ongoing monitoring and possible adjunctive therapy. Together, these interrelated procedures form the core of contemporary coronary intervention strategies aimed at relieving ischemic symptoms and improving cardiac outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 760
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass several interventional techniques used to restore patency of coronary vessels. Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) is classified as a specific type of both Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and interventional cardiac catheterization, reflecting its role as a catheter‑based method to dilate stenotic coronary arteries. Coronary thrombolysis is likewise a component of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and is considered a form of interventional cardiac catheterization; it serves as the parent concept for the broader procedure described as removal of coronary artery obstruction. The removal of coronary artery obstruction, which is linked to the Arabic term “رأف الوعاء,” is therefore understood as a specific application of coronary thrombolysis aimed at clearing occlusive material from the coronary lumen. Ptca is also associated with the term “رأف الوعاء” through a reference relationship, indicating that both ptca and removal of obstruction share the underlying goal of vessel reperfusion. Clinically, recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty can occur, highlighting the need for careful follow‑up and possible adjunctive therapies such as coronary thrombolysis or repeat obstruction removal. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the hierarchy and functional connections among catheter‑based coronary interventions, their therapeutic categories, and the potential post‑procedural complications that guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1136
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA), which is a specific coronary therapeutic procedure performed via interventional cardiac catheterization. PTCA is also described in Arabic as “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد,” reflecting its percutaneous nature. Interventional cardiac catheterization therefore includes both PTCA and coronary thrombolysis as its constituent techniques. Coronary thrombolysis is a method used for the removal of coronary artery obstruction and is classified as a subtype of interventional cardiac catheterization. The relationship between these modalities places PTCA and coronary thrombolysis under the broader category of coronary therapeutic services, while also recognizing coronary thrombolysis as a therapeutic approach aimed at clearing obstructed coronary vessels. Clinically, patients may experience recurrent angina after undergoing PTCA, highlighting the importance of monitoring and potential additional intervention. Together, these interconnected concepts illustrate the hierarchical organization of coronary interventions and their relevance to patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2211
retrieved_docs.content: Interventional cardiac catheterization is a broad category of minimally invasive cardiac procedures that includes percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA). PTCA, also referred to by the Arabic phrase “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد,” involves dilating a narrowed coronary artery using a balloon catheter introduced through the skin. Although PTCA is an effective revascularization technique, patients may experience recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, indicating that ongoing symptom monitoring and management are important components of post‑procedural care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2545
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substance is linked to a range of vascular interventional techniques. Open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents represents a surgical approach for delivering these agents directly into the arterial or venous system. Percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents by pulse spray technique provides a minimally invasive method that uses a spray of the therapeutic substance to achieve rapid distribution. Percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents by continuous infusion offers another minimally invasive option, delivering the agents steadily over time to maintain therapeutic levels. Transcatheter embolization of blood vessel is an additional catheter‑based procedure in which therapeutic or prophylactic substances are used to occlude a vessel, often to control bleeding or to treat vascular lesions. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse ways that injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substances can be administered through open, percutaneous, or transcatheter vascular access methods.

retrieved_docs.content: catheter placement, imaging guidance, and related interventions—uses medicinal products in both the initial vascular territory and any additional vascular territories. This underscores the central role of medicinal products across a spectrum of invasive neurological and vascular therapies, from structural drug classifications to the specific agents employed during complex procedural interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 762
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass a range of interventional techniques aimed at treating coronary artery disease. Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) is classified as a specific coronary therapeutic service and is also considered a form of interventional cardiac catheterization. PTCA is a child procedure of the broader intervention titled Removal of coronary artery obstruction by percutaneous transluminal balloon, single vessel, indicating that the balloon‑based removal technique includes PTCA as a component. Patients who undergo PTCA may experience recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, highlighting a potential post‑procedural complication. Coronary thrombolysis is another subset of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and is likewise a type of interventional cardiac catheterization. It serves as the parent category for both Removal of coronary artery obstruction (the general procedure) and the specific single‑vessel balloon removal technique, indicating that these obstruction‑removal methods are forms of thrombolytic therapy. Injection of coronary artery is identified as a specific instance of coronary thrombolysis and also as a specialized form of Injection of heart, linking it to broader cardiac injection practices. Both Injection of coronary artery and the single‑vessel balloon removal procedure are related to جراحة الشريان التاجي (coronary artery surgery), suggesting that they fall under the surgical management of coronary vessels. Overall, the knowledge network positions PTCA, various balloon‑based obstruction removal techniques, and coronary artery injections within the larger frameworks of coronary therapeutic services, coronary thrombolysis, and interventional cardiac catheterization, while also recognizing their association with coronary artery surgery and the clinical outcome of recurrent angina following PTCA.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 980
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) is a specific interventional cardiac catheterization technique used to remove coronary artery obstruction, most commonly performed with a percutaneous transluminal balloon in a single vessel. PTCA is classified as a child procedure of “Removal of coronary artery obstruction by percutaneous transluminal balloon, single vessel” and is encompassed within the broader category of “Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures.” In Arabic, PTCA is described as “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد,” emphasizing its percutaneous balloon approach. A notable clinical outcome is that recurrent angina can occur after PTCA, underscoring the importance of post‑procedure monitoring and management. Coronary thrombolysis represents a pharmacologic strategy for reperfusion that involves the injection of thrombolytic agents directly into the coronary artery. This injection of the coronary artery is a subtype of “Injection of heart” and is linked to the broader field of “جراحة الشريان التاجي” (coronary artery surgery). Coronary thrombolysis is also related to interventional cardiac catheterization and is considered a parent or broader category for procedures such as “Removal of coronary artery obstruction.” The relationship is further highlighted by the self‑classification of coronary thrombolysis, indicating its central role within its own therapeutic domain. Both PTCA and coronary thrombolysis are integral components of coronary therapeutic services, and they are interconnected through their association with interventional cardiac catheterization and coronary artery surgery. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians choose appropriate reperfusion strategies, anticipate complications such as recurrent angina after PTCA, and integrate mechanical and pharmacologic interventions for comprehensive management of coronary artery disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass both percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) and coronary thrombolysis as core interventional strategies for coronary artery disease. PTCA, a form of interventional cardiac catheterization, is performed to remove coronary artery obstruction by means of a percutaneous transluminal balloon in a single‑vessel setting; it is also described by the Arabic term “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد” and referenced simply as “رأب الوعاء.” Recurrent angina is a recognized clinical outcome that may occur after PTCA. Coronary thrombolysis, likewise classified within Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and regarded as a type of interventional cardiac catheterization, is conceptually linked to the injection of the coronary artery, which itself is a specific form of injection of the heart. The removal of coronary artery obstruction is related both to PTCA and to coronary thrombolysis, indicating that thrombolytic therapy can serve as a parent procedure for obstruction removal. These interrelationships highlight how PTCA, coronary thrombolysis, and associated injection techniques function together within the broader framework of coronary therapeutic interventions, informing patient management and the anticipation of post‑procedural complications such as recurrent angina.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2313
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substance is linked to several interventional techniques for delivering agents such as thrombolytics or chemotherapeutics. It can be performed through open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, through percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization using a pulse‑spray technique for the same types of agents, and via transcatheter embolization of a blood vessel. These procedural approaches illustrate the various catheter‑based methods by which therapeutic or prophylactic substances may be introduced into the vascular system to achieve targeted treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 927
retrieved_docs.content: Other vascular catheterization — not involving the heart — covers a range of arterial and venous catheter‑based techniques that are used for hemodynamic monitoring, drug delivery, and vessel embolization. Arterial cannulation with cardiac‑output monitoring, percutaneous insertion of catheters into the left radial artery for monitoring, and percutaneous insertion of catheters into the right femoral artery for monitoring are all classified as specific forms of this non‑cardiac vascular catheterization. Open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization that administers thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, as well as percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization that delivers the same agents by pulse‑spray technique or by continuous infusion, are also primary examples of other vascular catheterization. Transcatheter embolization of a blood vessel is another procedure that falls under this category. Each of these catheterization methods serves as a conduit for the injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substances, linking the procedural techniques directly to the delivery of drugs such as thrombolytics, chemotherapeutics, or other agents. Together, these interconnected procedures illustrate how non‑cardiac vascular catheterization provides essential pathways for monitoring circulatory parameters and administering targeted therapies in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) is classified within coronary therapeutic services and procedures and is also regarded as a form of coronary thrombolysis. It represents a specific technique for removal of coronary artery obstruction by a single‑vessel percutaneous transluminal balloon, belonging to interventional cardiac catheterization and to the broader category of cardiovascular surgical procedures (kardiovaskulární chirurgické výkony). The Arabic designations “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد” and “رأب الوعاء” are synonymous terms for PTCA, and patients may experience recurrent angina after the procedure, necessitating post‑procedural monitoring. Embolectomy and endarterectomy of the lower limbs constitute a distinct group of vascular surgical procedures on the lower limb, also placed under cardiovascular surgical procedures. This group includes femoropopliteal artery embolectomy with a catheter performed via a leg incision, illustrating a direct subtype

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1126
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial cannulization with cardiac output, open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents by pulse spray technique, percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents by continuous infusion, and transcatheter embolization of blood vessel are all classified under the broader category “other vascular catheterization; not heart.” This category also includes specific monitoring procedures such as percutaneous insertion of a catheter into the left axillary artery, left carotid artery, right carotid artery, right femoral artery, and right radial artery. Each of these catheterization techniques serves as a primary mapping for the respective procedural type, indicating that they are representative examples of non‑cardiac vascular catheter interventions. Moreover, injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substances is linked to each of these catheterization methods, reflecting that these procedures provide a route for delivering thrombolytic, chemotherapeutic, or other therapeutic agents. Together, these relationships illustrate how various non‑cardiac vascular catheterizations are organized, how specific arterial access sites fit within this framework, and how they facilitate the administration of therapeutic substances in clinical practice.
